name: Central circulation spine
description: A floor plan organized around a continuous circulation spine running along the building's main axis, with all rooms branching from it via doored walls. The spine is 2-3 blocks wide and works equally for medieval inns, modern apartments, Japanese ryokan, and Renaissance palaces — wherever rooms must connect coherently without dead-end pockets. Common rooms (kitchen, living, dining, great_hall) sit at the spine's center, private rooms at its ends, following Alexander's intimacy gradient. Vertical connectors (staircases) reserve their footprint ON the spine, never inside a habitable room, so multi-floor circulation stacks cleanly. Applicable to residential, tavern, temple, monument, and shop categories whenever the floor plate exceeds roughly 8×8 cells. The spine pattern is the simplest layout primitive that guarantees the voxel_connectivity invariant: every room reachable from every entry_point.
placement_rules: Lay a hallway corridor along the building's main axis, 2-3 cells wide, spanning ≥70% of the building length.
Common rooms (kitchen, living_room, dining_room, great_hall) attach to the spine's CENTER on either side.
Private rooms (bedroom, bathroom, study) attach to the spine's ENDS, away from the entry.
Every habitable room MUST share ≥3 wall cells with the spine — the door lives there.
Exterior entry_points open onto the entry_hall, which is the first cell of the spine.
When multi-floor, reserved_footprints for stairs sit ON the spine (never inside a habitable room).
